title: Slab-built octagonal trinket box
subtitle: Eight-sided slab box with a fitted lid and optional incised decoration
excerpt: An octagonal box assembled from eight angled slab panels joined at 135-degree angles, with a separate flat slab base and a fitted lid topped by a small pinched knob. Approximately 80 mm across and 60 mm tall. No kiln is needed. PVA sealing finishes the piece.
sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on slab-building methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness, used as the building unit for the box panels, base, and lid
The technique of scratching cross-hatch marks into both clay surfaces and applying slip before pressing them together to create a strong bond at a join
A creamy mixture of clay and water used as adhesive to join two clay surfaces; dry clay will not bond without it
The stage at which clay has stiffened enough to handle without deforming but is still slightly damp, allowing joins, trimming, and carving
The stage at which all moisture has left the clay and the piece is uniformly pale and ready for surface treatment
Fully dried but unfired clay work that is fragile and will dissolve if wetted before sealing
A join where two panels are cut at matching angles so that when pressed together they form a clean corner; for an octagon each edge is cut at 22.5 degrees

content.text: This box is assembled from eight
slab
panels joined at 135-degree interior angles using the
score-and-slip
method. A flat octagonal base and a fitting lid with a knob handle complete the form. No kiln is required. Finished dimensions are approximately 80 mm across the widest point and 60 mm tall.
Preparation
Cut a card template: draw an octagon 80 mm across (measured from flat edge to flat edge) and cut it out carefully. Each of the eight flat sides will be approximately 33 mm long. Prepare a
slip

by dissolving a small amount of clay in water to a thick cream consistency. Knead the clay for two minutes before rolling. Having all panels cut before assembly begins is important: joints must be made while all pieces are still soft, so cutting and joining one at a time risks the clay stiffening before the box is complete.
Construction

Roll the slab to 5 mm thickness. Use guide rails of equal height on either side of the rolling pin to maintain consistent thickness. Roll enough clay to cut ten pieces (eight side panels, one base, one lid) from a single sheet.
Cut the eight side panels. Each panel is a rectangle 33 mm wide and 60 mm tall. Cut eight identical rectangles with the fettling knife held against a straight edge. To create a
mitre join
, bevel each long edge at 22.5 degrees by angling the fettling knife as you cut. This ensures the eight panels close into a flush octagon when assembled.
Cut the base. Place the octagon card template on the slab and cut around it with the fettling knife. The base should be 5 mm thick. Set the base on the wooden board as the assembly platform.

Assemble the box. Work with two panels at a time. Score the bevelled edges of adjacent panels and the bottom edge of each panel using the needle tool. Apply slip to all scored surfaces. Press the first two panels together at the mitred angle and hold for 30 seconds. Stand them vertically against a small jar or cup to keep them upright while you work on the next pair. Add panels one at a time, working around the octagon. Ensure each join closes completely with no gaps.

Attach the panel ring to the base. Score the bottom edge of all eight assembled panels and the outer edge of the base. Apply slip to both surfaces, then lower the panel ring onto the base and press firmly. Smooth the join around the outside with a damp finger. Do not smooth the interior base join yet.

Reinforce the interior. Once the box is standing, run a thin coil of fresh clay around all eight interior corner joins and the base-to-wall join. Press the coil firmly into each corner with a fingertip or the back of the needle tool. Smooth the coil into the join to create an internal fillet that prevents cracking at the corners during drying.

Make the lid. Cut an octagonal slab 3 mm larger than the octagon template on each side (approximately 86 mm across) so the lid overlaps the box walls by 3 mm. Make a knob by rolling a 20 mm ball of clay and pinching one end flatter. Score and slip the flat end of the knob and the centre of the lid top surface; press together and smooth the join. Allow the lid to dry flat on the board.
Add incised decoration at the
leather-hard

stage. Use the sgraffito tool or needle tool to incise geometric lines on the lid surface and side panels. Geometric patterns such as hatching, diamonds, or concentric octagons work well with the box's angular form.
Drying
Allow the box to dry fully at room temperature. The thick joins and interior fillets mean drying takes 48-3 days to reach the
bone-dry
stage. Do not rush drying with a heat source. Keep the box in the same position throughout drying to prevent uneven shrinkage across the joins. The
greenware
piece is at its most fragile around the corner joins. Handle it by the base rather than the walls. Dry the lid separately.
Finishing

Sand exterior corners lightly with fine sandpaper if any joins are slightly raised. Paint with acrylic paint, applying thin coats. Allow paint to dry for two hours. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to all exterior surfaces and the interior base. Allow each coat to dry before applying the next. Sand lightly between coats for a smoother finish if desired.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. This box is not suitable as a water vessel. It is suitable for storing dry items: jewellery, small accessories, dried botanicals. Do not store damp items inside. Wipe with a dry cloth. Keep indoors away from humidity. Do not drop the box: the corner joins are the weakest points.
Variations
Smaller jewellery box: scale the template down to 50 mm across and cut panels 40 mm tall for a smaller, shallower ring box.
Stamp-decorated panels: stamp each panel with a repeating motif before assembly. The finished box will have a patterned border all the way around.
Hinged lid: omit the knob. Pierce two holes in the back panel and two matching holes in the lid. Thread a length of leather cord through all four holes to make a simple cord hinge.

rows.problem: Corners gap open during or after drying
rows.cause: Insufficient slip at the join or bevels did not fit flush
rows.fix: At leather-hard, fill the gap with fresh slip and press closed. At bone-dry, fill with undiluted PVA, allow to cure, then sand flush

rows.cause: Panels were not all the same height, or base is not level
rows.fix: At leather-hard, press the leaning wall gently vertical and support it with a wad of scrunched paper inside the box while it finishes drying

rows.cause: The lid warped during drying on an uneven surface
rows.fix: At leather-hard, press the lid flat on a board under a light weight. If bone-dry, sand the underside of the lid rim very gently with fine sandpaper

rows.problem: Interior fillet pulls away from the corner
rows.cause: Fresh coil clay was added to clay that had already started to stiffen without score-and-slip
rows.fix: Score the corner surface and the back of the fillet coil, apply slip, and press back into place. Work within two hours of the initial assembly

rows.cause: The clay was too dry when cut
rows.fix: Roll fresh clay and allow it to rest covered for five minutes before cutting. Keep cut pieces covered with a damp cloth while preparing others
